nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes and clear lagoon reefs, typically in areas of mixed living and dead coral with numerous shelter holes and passages; recorded from 1–56 m depth.

diet.notes: Best fed a varied marine diet that includes spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations, and meaty foods such as enriched frozen brine and mysis shrimp. In a mature aquarium with live rock they will graze opportunistically throughout the day; feeding 2–3 times daily is generally advised.

summary: The True Shepard's Angelfish (Centropyge shepardi) is a rarely-collected dwarf angelfish from the Mariana and Ogasawara Islands, recognizable by its bright orange-yellow body and electric-blue vertical barring on the upper half. Generally regarded as reef-safe with caution.
careNotes: The True Shepard's Angelfish, also sold as the Mango Angelfish, is a dwarf angelfish from the remote Western Pacific that does best in a mature, well-established reef aquarium. It is generally advised to introduce this species after more passive fish are already settled in, which helps reduce territorial pressure. Plenty of live rock with shelter holes and open swimming space is strongly recommended. Reef compatibility is generally good when the fish is well fed; isolated cases of nipping at LPS (large-polyp stony) coral polyps and clam mantles have been reported. This species is protogynous — meaning individuals begin life as females and the dominant fish transitions to male — and naturally forms small harems in the wild. In the aquarium, keepers generally maintain them singly or as a male-female pair rather than housing multiple conspecifics together. Like most dwarf angels, the True Shepard's Angelfish can be sensitive to copper-based medications at higher therapeutic doses.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely achieved and is considered an advanced undertaking. The True Shepard's Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems in the wild, with the dominant individual transitioning to male. The species spawns pelagically — releasing eggs and sperm into the open water column — and larval rearing is difficult enough that it has not been established at hobbyist scale.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), adult size (FishBase records 9.0 cm TL max vs LiveAquaria 3.5–4 in / 9–10 cm — minor disagreement, used a conservative 9–11 cm range), depth range (1–56 m), native range (Mariana and Ogasawara Islands; possible population SW of Palau), and habitat. IUCN status Least Concern.

additional.name: AquariumCodex / aquarium hobby care guides
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check for tank size. Hobby guides span 55 gal (~210 L) to 500 L recommendations; 70 gal/~265 L is the most commonly cited minimum. Rounded up to 300 L here to account for dwarf-angel territoriality and the need for open swimming space.

fish.breedingNotes: A protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems in the wild, with the dominant individual transitioning to male. Captive breeding is very rarely achieved.
